items.correct: By the word's grammatical function in a sentence — not its meaning. The same word can belong to different classes depending on use (e.g. 'run' as noun or verb).
items.misconception: A noun is always a person, place or thing.
items.random_wrong: A noun is any word that starts with a capital letter.
items.why: 'Running' can be a noun ('Running is healthy'), a verb ('I am running'), or an adjective ('running water'). The notional definition fails — function in context determines word class.

items.q: What is the subject of a sentence?
items.correct: The noun phrase that performs the action of the verb (in active voice) or that the verb is predicated about — not necessarily what the sentence is 'about'.
items.misconception: The subject is whatever the sentence is talking about.
items.random_wrong: The subject is always the first word in the sentence.
items.why: In 'The cake was eaten by John', the subject is 'the cake' (grammatically), though the sentence is 'about' John. Students conflate topic with grammatical subject.

items.correct: A prestige accent associated historically with public schools and the BBC — spoken by a small minority of Britons; it is an accent, not a dialect.
items.misconception: RP is the correct way to speak English.
items.random_wrong: RP is the accent everyone in southern England uses.
items.why: RP has high social prestige but no inherent correctness. Descriptive linguistics treats it as one accent among many — students absorb the prescriptive notion of 'correct' speech.

items.q: A story told from the perspective of 'I went into the room' is in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1st person. “2nd person” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which method is best described as 'choice of part of speech'?
items.why: Correct answer: Word class. “Imagery” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many main narrative person choices exist (1st, 2nd, 3rd)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
